words.definition_en: a strong need that someone feels to keep taking a drug or doing something harmful
words.example: His addiction to video games is affecting his schoolwork.

words.definition_en: to love someone very much and feel very proud of them
words.example: I absolutely adore chocolate.

words.definition_en: a feeling of excitement about something that is going to happen
words.definition_vi: sự mong đợi, sự dự đoán
words.example: There was a lot of anticipation before the concert.

words.definition_en: to be attractive or interesting
words.example: The idea of a vacation really appeals to me.

words.definition_en: decided without any reason or plan
words.example: The decision was completely arbitrary.

words.definition_en: something that you hope to achieve
words.example: Her aspiration is to become a doctor.

words.definition_en: to accept something that is difficult or unpleasant
words.example: I can't bear the heat.

words.definition_en: something that must be done because of a law or rule
words.example: Math is a compulsory subject.

words.definition_en: happy and satisfied
words.example: She was content with her life.

words.definition_en: a very strong feeling of wanting something
words.example: I had a sudden craving for ice cream.

words.definition_en: to refuse politely to accept or to do something
words.example: I offered to help, but she declined.

words.definition_en: believing that something is very important and giving a lot of time and energy to it
words.example: She is a dedicated teacher.

words.definition_en: to give someone great pleasure or satisfaction
words.definition_vi: làm vui lòng, làm thích thú
words.example: The new toy delighted the children.

words.definition_en: a strong hope or wish
words.example: I have a strong desire to travel.

words.definition_en: to give most of your time, energy, etc. to someone or something
words.example: He devoted his life to helping the poor.

words.definition_en: to recognize or show that two things are not the same
words.example: It's hard to differentiate between the two types of wine.

words.definition_en: to wish that you had someone else's possessions, abilities, etc.
words.example: I envy your success.

words.definition_en: to want to have or do something
words.example: Do you fancy going out tonight?

words.definition_en: the state of being very interested in something
words.definition_vi: sự mê hoặc, sự quyến rũ
words.example: He has a fascination with ancient history.

words.definition_en: to prefer someone or something
words.example: The teacher favours the younger students.

words.definition_en: wanting more of something than you need
words.example: Don't be greedy, leave some for the others.

words.definition_en: a sudden strong wish to do something
words.example: I bought the dress on impulse.

words.definition_en: wanting to do something or likely to do something
words.example: I'm inclined to agree with you.

words.definition_en: a feeling of enjoying or liking something
words.example: He has a liking for hot food.

words.definition_en: not very good; of only average quality
words.example: The movie was only mediocre.

words.definition_en: the reason for doing something
words.example: The police are looking for a motive.

words.definition_en: ordinary and not interesting or exciting
words.example: I'm tired of my mundane job.

words.definition_en: thinking about someone or something all the time
words.example: She is obsessed with her weight.

words.definition_en: something that is optional is available but you do not have to do it or use it
words.example: The course is optional.

words.definition_en: a very strong belief or feeling about something
words.example: Music is her passion.

words.definition_en: to say that you admire someone or something
words.example: The teacher praised the student's work.

words.definition_en: to make a firm decision to do something
words.example: I resolved to work harder.

words.definition_en: to give up something important in order to get something else
words.example: He sacrificed his career for his family.

words.definition_en: to make a great effort to achieve something
words.example: We must strive for better results.

words.definition_en: the kind of things that you like
words.example: She has very good taste in clothes.

words.definition_en: something that is tempting seems very good and you would like to have it or do it
words.example: The cake looks very tempting.

words.definition_en: to strongly suggest that someone does something
words.example: I urge you to reconsider.

words.definition_en: to say that you are pleased to accept something
words.example: We welcome your suggestions.

words.definition_en: useful, important, or good enough to be a suitable reward for the money or time spent or the effort made
words.example: It's a worthwhile cause.

words.definition_en: to want something very much, especially when it is very difficult to get
words.example: I yearn for a quiet life.

words.definition_en: very interesting and keeping your attention completely
words.example: I found the book completely absorbing.

words.definition_en: relaxed and not formal
words.example: He was wearing casual clothes.

words.definition_en: making you feel extremely happy and excited
words.example: Skiing is an exhilarating experience.

words.definition_en: a feeling of being extremely tired
words.example: The soldiers were suffering from fatigue.

words.definition_en: not working or being used
words.example: The machines have been idle for a week.

words.definition_en: to allow yourself to have or do something that you enjoy
words.example: Go on, indulge yourself!

words.definition_en: the way that you live
words.example: He has a very healthy lifestyle.

words.definition_en: time that you are allowed to spend away from work
words.example: I'm on leave next week.

words.definition_en: a short trip made by a group of people for pleasure
words.definition_vi: cuộc dã ngoại, chuyến đi chơi
words.example: We went on a family outing to the beach.

words.definition_en: an activity that you enjoy doing when you are not working
words.example: Reading is my favourite pastime.

words.definition_en: to follow or try to achieve something over a period of time
words.example: She is pursuing a career in law.

words.definition_en: activities that you do for pleasure
words.example: The park is used for recreation.

words.definition_en: a short period of rest from something unpleasant or difficult
words.definition_vi: thời gian nghỉ ngơi, tạm hoãn
words.example: The weekend provided a brief respite from work.

words.definition_en: spending a lot of time sitting down
words.example: He has a sedentary job.

words.definition_en: to spend time with other people in a friendly way
words.example: He likes to socialise with his colleagues.

words.definition_en: the state of being alone, especially when you find this pleasant
words.example: She enjoyed the solitude of the forest.

words.definition_en: boring and continuing for too long
words.example: The lecture was incredibly tedious.

words.definition_en: not very important, serious, or valuable
words.example: It was just a trivial matter.

words.definition_en: to relax after a period of work or tension
words.example: I like to unwind with a good book.

words.definition_en: a place where an organized meeting, concert, etc. takes place
words.example: The stadium is the perfect venue for the concert.

words.definition_en: if something centres around someone or something, they are its main subject
words.example: The story centres around a young girl and her dog.

words.definition_en: start enjoying something or become enthusiastic about it
words.example: I'm really getting into jazz these days.

words.definition_en: enjoy a particular thing or activity
words.example: I don't go in for team sports.

words.definition_en: stop liking someone or something
words.example: I've gone off coffee lately.

words.definition_en: if something or someone grows on you, you start to like them more
words.example: I didn't like the song at first, but it's growing on me.

words.definition_en: spend time in a particular place or with particular people
words.definition_vi: đi chơi, dành thời gian tại
words.example: We used to hang out at the mall.

words.definition_en: continue to do something
words.example: You're doing a great job, keep up the good work!

words.definition_en: relax and enjoy yourself, doing no work
words.example: We spent the weekend lazing around the pool.

words.definition_en: spend time doing things in a relaxed way
words.definition_vi: làm việc linh tinh, chơi bời
words.example: Stop messing about and do your homework!

words.definition_en: learn a new skill or start a habit without intending to
words.definition_vi: học lỏm, bắt đầu một thói quen
words.example: I picked up a few words of Spanish while on holiday.

words.definition_en: spend a particular amount of time or effort doing something
words.example: She's put in a lot of work on this project.

words.definition_en: find someone or something by looking for them in a determined way
words.example: I sought out the quietest spot in the park.

words.definition_en: include something
words.example: The tour takes in several historic sites.

words.definition_en: have a particular amount of time away from work
words.example: I'm taking two weeks off in July.

words.definition_en: take someone to a place like a cinema and usually pay for them
words.example: He took her out for dinner.

words.definition_en: prepare for a sport or activity by doing gentle exercises
words.example: It's important to warm up before you exercise.

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'choice'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'choice'
words.example: make a choice, exercise choice, have no choice, choice between, choice of, personal/parental/consumer choice, informed choice, wide choice, obvious choice

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'choose'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'choose'
words.example: choose from, choose between, choose sb/sth as, choose sb/sth out of, choose to do, pick and choose, nothing/little to choose between

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'decide'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'decide'
words.example: decide to do, decide against/in favour of, decide that, decide between, decide for yourself, decide on

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'delight'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'delight'
words.example: delight in doing, take (great) delight in doing, to sb's delight, with/in delight

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'eager'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'eager'
words.example: eager to do, eager for, only too eager to do

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'free'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'free'
words.example: set sb free, let sb go free, free sb from, walk free, free to do, free from, free and easy, free time

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'impulse'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'impulse'
words.example: act on impulse, do sth on impulse, control/resist an impulse, an impulse to do

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'keen'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'keen'
words.example: keen (for sb) to do, keen on, keen interest in

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'leisure'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'leisure'
words.example: have the leisure to do, at your leisure, leisure centre, leisure pursuits, leisure time

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'like'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'like'
words.example: like (sb) to do, like doing, like it when, just like, anything/nothing like, it's not like sb to do, like crazy/mad, like so, something like

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'need'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'need'
words.example: need (sb) to do, need do, need doing, meet a need, have no need of, in need (of)

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'off'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'off'
words.example: have/take/be given the day off, have/take/be given time off, off work/college, off sick

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'opt'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'opt'
words.example: opt for, opt to do

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'option'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'option'
words.example: have no option (but to do), consider your options, the option of doing, the option to do, keep/leave your options open

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'play'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'play'
words.example: play against/for, play at, play by yourself, play for time, play the fool, play sth by ear, at play

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'praise'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'praise'
words.example: praise sb for doing, win/earn/deserve praise, full of praise for, in praise of

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'prefer'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'prefer'
words.example: prefer sth to, prefer (sb) to do, prefer doing, would prefer (that), would prefer it if

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'rest'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'rest'
words.example: rest sth on/against, rest assured that, take/have a rest (from), get some rest, come to rest, at rest

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'taste'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'taste'
words.example: develop/have a taste for, in good/bad taste, sense of taste, acquired taste

words.definition_en: Patterns and collocations for 'want'
words.definition_vi: Các cấu trúc và từ đi kèm với 'want'
words.example: want (sb) to do, want sth done, for want of

words.definition_en: with nothing in particular to do
words.definition_vi: rảnh rỗi, không có việc gì làm
words.example: If you're at a loose end this weekend, why not come over?

words.definition_en: someone who spends a lot of time sitting at home watching television
words.definition_vi: người lười vận động, chỉ thích ngồi xem TV
words.example: He's become a bit of a couch potato since he bought that new TV.

words.definition_en: do because of a sudden feeling that you must have or do something
words.definition_vi: làm gì đó bộc phát/tùy hứng
words.example: I decided to go to Paris on a whim.

words.definition_en: have more time available than you need
words.definition_vi: có quá nhiều thời gian rảnh
words.example: Now that the kids are at school, I have a lot of time on my hands.

words.definition_en: relax and enjoy yourself because you are in a comfortable environment
words.example: It's the weekend, let your hair down!

words.word: life and soul of the party
words.definition_en: someone who is very lively at social events
words.example: He's always the life and soul of the party.
collocations: be the life and soul of the party

words.definition_en: sit down and relax, especially with your feet raised off the ground
words.example: Go and put your feet up for a while.

words.definition_en: rest after being very busy, so that you will be ready to start working again
words.example: I need a holiday to recharge my batteries.

words.definition_en: decide that you want something very much
words.definition_vi: quyết tâm đạt được cái gì
words.example: She's set her heart on becoming a doctor.

words.word: variety is the spice of life
words.definition_en: used for saying that different experiences and situations make life enjoyable or interesting
words.definition_vi: thay đổi là hương vị của cuộc sống
words.example: I like trying new things – variety is the spice of life!

words.definition_en: Forms of 'available'
words.example: available, (un)availability, unavailable

words.definition_en: Forms of 'choose'
words.example: choose, chose, chosen, choosy

words.definition_en: Forms of 'desire'
words.example: desire, (un)desirable, desirous, desired, (un)desirably

words.definition_en: Forms of 'do'
words.example: do, overdo, outdo, redo, undo, doing, doings

words.definition_en: Forms of 'envy'
words.example: envy, (un)enviable, (un)enviably, envious(ly)

words.definition_en: Forms of 'leisure'
words.example: leisure, leisured, leisurely

words.definition_en: Forms of 'motive'
words.example: motive, (de)motivated, (de)motivator, (de)motivation, (de)motivating, motivational

words.definition_en: Forms of 'obsess'
words.example: obsess, obsession, obsessed, obsessive(ly)

words.definition_en: Forms of 'play'
words.example: play, replay, overplay, downplay, player, playful(ly)

words.definition_en: Forms of 'prefer'
words.example: prefer, preference, preferable, preferred, preferential, preferably

words.definition_en: Forms of 'pursue'
words.example: pursue, pursuit, pursuer

words.definition_en: Forms of 'relax'
words.example: relax, relaxation, relaxing, relaxed

words.definition_en: Forms of 'rest'
words.example: rest, restlessness, unrest, restive, restful(ly), restless(ly)

words.definition_en: Forms of 'rhythm'
words.example: rhythm, rhythmic(al), rhythmically

words.definition_en: Forms of 'satisfy'
words.example: satisfy, dissatisfy, (un)satisfactory, (dis)satisfied, (un)satisfying(ly), satisfactorily

words.definition_en: Forms of 'select'
words.example: select, deselect, selection, selective(ly)

words.definition_en: Forms of 'sport'
words.example: sport, sportsman/woman/person, sportsmanship, sports, sporting, sporty

words.definition_en: Forms of 'stimulate'
words.example: stimulate, stimulation, stimulant, stimulus, stimuli, stimulating, stimulated

words.definition_en: Forms of 'tend'
words.example: tend, tendency

words.definition_en: Forms of 'zeal'
words.example: zeal, zealot, zealous(ly)

items.sentence: Although she would have preferred to carry on working, my mum ___ her career in order to have children.
items.explanation: To 'sacrifice' a career means to give it up for something else considered more important.

items.sentence: I find the offer quite ___ , but I think I'd rather study at Oxford.
items.explanation: A 'tempting' offer is one that is attractive and makes you want to accept it.

items.sentence: I don't normally like noisy clubs, but I had a sudden ___ to see what the Blue Parrot was like.
items.explanation: An 'impulse' is a sudden strong desire to do something without thinking about the results.

items.sentence: Jerry loves snowboarding so much that it's almost like a drug ___ .
items.explanation: An 'addiction' is a strong need that someone has to have something or do something, like a drug.

items.sentence: I don't want to do the course in applied statistics, but it's ___ .
items.explanation: 'Compulsory' means that something must be done because of a rule or law.

items.sentence: Don't worry about me - I'm quite ___ to sit here and wait for you to come back.
items.explanation: To be 'content' means to be happy and satisfied with what you have or with a situation.

items.sentence: When I was pregnant, I often got a sudden ___ for tinned sardines.
items.explanation: A 'craving' is a very strong feeling of wanting something, especially a particular type of food.

items.sentence: Thank you for thinking of us, but I'm afraid we're going to have to ___ your kind invitation.
items.explanation: To 'decline' an invitation is a formal and polite way of saying that you cannot accept it.

items.sentence: I've never seen anyone so ___ to their job as Philip is.
items.explanation: 'Dedicated' means spending a lot of time and effort on something because you think it is important.

items.sentence: Why do you have such a ___ with model railways?
items.explanation: 'Fascination' is the state of being very interested in something or attracted by something.

items.sentence: I wish you would stop wasting so much time on computer games and do something a little more ___ .
items.explanation: 'Worthwhile' means useful, important, or good enough to be worth the amount of time or money that it costs.

items.sentence: Olivia has always ___ to return to the country she was born in.
items.explanation: To 'yearn' means to want something very much, especially when it is very difficult to get.

exercises.label: B
exercises.instruction: Complete using the correct form of the words in the box.

items.sentence: Trisha suggested going to Indonesia, but the idea didn't really ___ to me.
items.explanation: To 'appeal' to someone means to be attractive or interesting to them.

items.sentence: Young children seem to ___ in showing things they've made at school to their parents.
items.explanation: To 'delight in doing something' means to get a lot of pleasure from doing something.

items.sentence: Francis obviously ___ his three children and will do anything for them.
items.explanation: To 'adore' someone means to love them very much.

items.sentence: I think I just ___ getting a DVD and staying in tonight.
items.explanation: To 'fancy doing something' means to want to do it.

items.sentence: The firefighters ___ Mrs Collins for her quick thinking when the fire broke out.
items.explanation: To 'praise' someone means to say that they have done something very well.

items.sentence: We would ___ anyone who values personal freedom to vote against this proposal.
items.explanation: To 'urge' someone to do something means to strongly suggest that they should do it.

items.sentence: The Prime Minister ___ the suggestion from the leader of the opposition and said that the government would consider it carefully.
items.explanation: To 'welcome' a suggestion means to say that you are pleased about it.

items.sentence: I don't know how Liz can ___ to work at that place.
items.explanation: To 'bear to do something' means to be able to accept or do something unpleasant.

items.sentence: It's important to have clear goals to ___ towards.
items.explanation: To 'strive towards something' means to make a lot of effort to achieve it.

items.sentence: Parents should try to treat all their children equally and not ___ one over another.
items.explanation: To 'favour' someone means to treat them better than others.

items.sentence: It's quite easy to ___ between margarine and butter because butter has a creamier taste.
items.explanation: To 'differentiate between' two things means to see or show the difference between them.

items.sentence: The Board of Directors has ___ to increase sales by at least 20% over the coming year.
items.explanation: To 'resolve' to do something means to make a firm decision to do it.

items.sentence: Share your chocolate with your brother and don't be so ___ .
items.explanation: 'Greedy' means wanting more than you need.

items.sentence: Claudia's completely ___ with Jack and doesn't talk about anything else these days.
items.explanation: To be 'obsessed with' someone means to think about them all the time.

items.sentence: I smiled in ___ as the waiter brought the desserts to our table.
items.explanation: 'Anticipation' is a feeling of excitement about something that is going to happen.

items.sentence: Joining the Air Force has always been an ___ of mine.
items.explanation: An 'aspiration' is something that you want to achieve.

items.sentence: These two day trips are included in the price of the tour, while the third is ___ and costs 50 euros extra.
items.explanation: 'Optional' means that you can choose whether to do it or not.

items.sentence: We don't have any information about the hotels, so we'll just have to make an ___ decision.
items.explanation: An 'arbitrary' decision is one that is made without any good reason.

items.sentence: If you look at Madeleine's house, it's obvious she has a lot of ___ .
items.explanation: If someone has 'taste', they have a good judgment of what is attractive.

items.sentence: I've always had a ___ for Naomi, ever since she moved in next door.
items.explanation: To have a 'liking for' someone means to like them.

items.sentence: Life in this small town is really quite ___ and I can't wait to move to the big city.
items.explanation: 'Mundane' means ordinary and not interesting or exciting.

items.sentence: I thought her latest book was ___ compared to the others, which I really enjoyed.
items.explanation: 'Mediocre' means not very good in quality.

items.sentence: Eva got everything she had ever ___ when she married a rich businessman.
items.explanation: To 'desire' something means to want it very much.

items.sentence: I ___ people who can just walk into a room full of strangers and feel at home.
items.explanation: To 'envy' someone means to wish that you had their qualities or possessions.

exercises.label: D
exercises.instruction: If the word in bold is correct, put a tick. If it is incorrect, replace it with one of the words in bold from the other sentences.

items.sentence: It should be quite a ___ event, so don't dress up too much.
items.explanation: 'Casual' (from sentence 8) means relaxed and not formal, suitable for an event where you don't need to dress up.

items.sentence: Without enough leisure time, people begin to suffer from stress and ___ .
items.explanation: 'Fatigue' (from sentence 6) is a feeling of being extremely tired, which often results from stress.

items.sentence: I get 25 days a year ___ , which is probably about average.
items.explanation: 'Leave' (from sentence 10) refers to time allowed away from work.

items.sentence: The new Arts Centre should be a great ___ for concerts.
items.explanation: 'Venue' (from sentence 3) is a place where an organized event like a concert happens.

items.sentence: Last time I had a day off, we went on a family ___ to the beach.
items.explanation: 'Outing' (from sentence 4) is a short pleasure trip.

items.sentence: Kids just wouldn't fit in with my ___ , so I don't plan to have any at the moment.
items.explanation: 'Lifestyle' (from sentence 5) is the way you live.

items.sentence: I like to be quite active when I've got time off because my job is quite ___ .
items.explanation: 'Sedentary' (from sentence 1) means spending a lot of time sitting down.

items.sentence: I find nothing so relaxing as reading a really ___ book.
items.explanation: 'Absorbing' (from sentence 7) means very interesting and keeping your attention completely.

items.sentence: Work's so ___ that I can't wait to meet my friends in the evening.
items.explanation: 'Tedious' (from sentence 9) is correct; it means boring and continuing for too long.

items.sentence: The most relaxing things about getting away to the mountains are the peace and ___ .
items.explanation: 'Solitude' (from sentence 2) is the state of being completely alone, especially when this is pleasant or relaxing.

exercises.label: E
exercises.instruction: Complete using the correct form of the words in the box.

items.sentence: During the first half of the nineteenth century, more and more people were employed in factories, working 14- or 15-hour days that left them little time for hobbies, sport or any other form of (1) ___
items.explanation: 'Recreation' refers to things that you do for enjoyment when you are not working.

items.sentence: ___ and meant they weren't able to (2) ___ with friends.
items.explanation: To 'socialise' means to spend time with other people for enjoyment.

items.sentence: A key breakthrough in Britain came with the Ten Hour Act (1847), which limited the working day and ensured that workers got some (3) ___ from their jobs.
items.explanation: 'Respite' is a short period of rest from something unpleasant or difficult.

items.sentence: People now had at least some time to (4) ___ from work at the end of the day
items.explanation: To 'unwind' means to relax after a period of work or tension.

items.sentence: and (5) ___ themselves.
items.explanation: To 'indulge' oneself means to allow oneself to have or do something that you enjoy.

items.sentence: Many members of the middle class hated the idea of the workers being (6) ___
items.explanation: To be 'idle' means not working or being used.

items.sentence: and believed that they would waste their time on (7) ___ matters
items.explanation: 'Trivial' means not very important, serious, or valuable.

items.sentence: instead of (8) ___ productive activities, such as education or going to church.
items.explanation: To 'pursue' an activity means to follow it or be involved in it.

items.sentence: The workers, however, found the new freedom (9) ___
items.explanation: 'Exhilarating' means making you feel extremely happy and excited.

items.sentence: and threw themselves into new (10) ___ with enthusiasm.
items.explanation: A 'pastime' is an activity that you do for enjoyment when you are not working.

items.sentence: I suppose I spend a lot of time just hanging ___
items.explanation: 'hanging out' means spending time in a particular place or with particular people.

items.sentence: The best thing about camping is just messing ___
items.explanation: 'messing about' means spending time doing things in a relaxed way.

items.sentence: Since I'm self-employed, I'm able to take ___
items.explanation: 'take ... off' means to have a particular amount of time away from work.

items.sentence: Drew spends a lot of time seeking ___
items.explanation: 'seeking out' means finding someone or something by looking for them in a determined way.

items.sentence: Most of my leisure time seems to centre ___
items.explanation: 'centre around' means to have something as the main subject of attention or interest.

items.sentence: The tour we're going on next week takes ___
items.explanation: 'takes in' means to include something.

items.sentence: I'm not one of those people who go in ___
items.explanation: 'go in for' means to enjoy a particular thing or activity.

items.sentence: With a bit of practice, I picked ___
items.explanation: 'picked up' means to learn a new skill without intending to.

exercises.label: G
exercises.instruction: Complete using a phrasal verb with a word from box A in the right form and a word from box B. You need to use one word from box B more than once.

items.sentence: I don't have much money these days, so it's hard to ___ my music collection.
items.explanation: 'Keep up' means to continue to do something or maintain something.

items.sentence: If you're ___ Amanda ___ this weekend, why don't you go to the Popwave festival?
items.explanation: 'Take out' means to take someone to a place like a cinema or restaurant and pay for them.

items.sentence: I didn't like this CD at first, but now I'm really ___ it.
items.explanation: 'Get into' means to start enjoying something or become enthusiastic about it.

items.sentence: Carl and I just spent the whole day ___ listening to music.
items.explanation: 'Laze around' means to relax and enjoy yourself, doing no work.

items.sentence: I'm still not sure about this track, but I think it's starting to ___ me.
items.explanation: If something 'grows on' you, you start to like it more.

items.sentence: Before the concert started, we could hear the band ___ off stage.
items.explanation: 'Warm up' means to prepare for an activity by practicing just before it starts.

items.sentence: I used to love this band, but I've ___ them since the lead singer left.
items.explanation: 'Go off' means to stop liking someone or something.

items.sentence: You can tell they've ___ a lot of hard work when you listen to their latest album.
items.explanation: 'Put in' means to spend a particular amount of time or effort doing something.

exercises.label: H
exercises.instruction: The phrasal verb get into has a meaning connected to the idea of becoming involved in an activity or situation. Tick the sentences where the phrasal verb has a meaning connected to a similar idea.

items.sentence: I love watching TV, but it does bite into my free time.
items.explanation: 'Bite into' here means to use up a part of something, not becoming involved in an activity.

items.sentence: Jordan was relieved when she finally broke into the music business.
items.explanation: 'Break into' here means to become involved in a new type of business or activity.

items.sentence: When you're flying a model aeroplane, the important thing is not to crash into anything!
items.explanation: 'Crash into' means a physical collision.

items.sentence: The council have entered into discussions on building a new sports centre in town.
items.explanation: 'Enter into' means to start to become involved in something such as an agreement or a discussion.

items.sentence: To access the online games network, key your username and password into the box below.
items.explanation: 'Key into' means to put information into a computer using a keyboard.

items.sentence: Think carefully before you rush into such a long-term agreement.
items.explanation: 'Rush into' means to start doing something without thinking about it enough first.

items.sentence: Kelly launched into a detailed account of her plans for the summer.
items.explanation: 'Launch into' means to start saying or doing something with a lot of energy.

items.sentence: Pete is always taking up new hobbies and he really throws himself into them.
items.explanation: 'Throw oneself into' means to start doing something with a lot of enthusiasm and energy.

exercises.label: I
exercises.instruction: One word in each sentence is incorrect. Underline the incorrect word and write the correct word.

items.sentence: It's the boss's birthday next Wednesday, and there's a rumour we might be taken the day off.
items.explanation: The correct passive construction is 'be given the day off'.

items.sentence: I know you'd rather I took up a sport, but it's not your choice to do.
items.explanation: The standard collocation is 'make a choice'.

items.sentence: Some of the courses are compulsory, so we can't just pick and select whatever we like.
items.explanation: The fixed idiom is 'pick and choose'.

items.sentence: If you won't give me a pay rise, then I shall have to think my options.
items.explanation: The correct phrase is 'consider your options' or 'think about your options'.

items.sentence: The council said they had no option apart to close the sports centre.
items.explanation: The phrase 'have no option but to' is used to show there is no other choice.

items.sentence: Angela travels a lot and is only very eager to experience new places.
items.explanation: The intensive phrase is 'only too eager', meaning 'very eager'.

items.sentence: Jake's been practising as mad to impress the others in the band.
items.explanation: The idiom is 'like mad' (or 'like crazy').

items.sentence: I can't believe the judge just let the accused be free like that.
items.explanation: The correct phrase is 'let someone go free'.

items.sentence: I've decided in favour for joining the local swimming team.
items.explanation: The correct preposition is 'in favour of'.

items.sentence: You should plan things more carefully and not just act with impulse all the time.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'act on impulse'.

items.sentence: There is no doubt technology and shorter working hours have (1) got / let / set people free to enjoy their leisure time more than ever before.
items.explanation: The phrase 'set someone free' means to release them from constraints.

items.sentence: Many people (2) do / have / make the leisure to pursue hobbies and other activities they (3) like / prefer / want to the work routine.
items.explanation: The phrase is 'have the leisure to do something'.

items.sentence: ___activities they (3) like / prefer / want to the work routine.
items.explanation: The construction 'prefer A to B' is used for comparison.

items.sentence: What leisure activities will people (4) choose / decide / opt for in the future?
items.explanation: The phrasal verb is 'opt for'.

items.sentence: We can expect to see increased personal (5) choice / decision / option.
items.explanation: 'Personal choice' is a standard collocation.

items.sentence: The range of leisure (6) actions / hobbies / pursuits will expand___
items.explanation: 'Leisure pursuits' is the formal term for activities done in free time.

items.sentence: ___and people will have the (7) decision / option / preference of following up an interest in almost any activity.
items.explanation: 'Have the option of doing something' is a standard phrase.

items.sentence: Whether at home or at (8) enjoyment / leisure / relaxation centres, there will be an ever-increasing need to provide for people at (9) play / recreation / rest.
items.explanation: 'Leisure centre' is a standard term for a public building where people can do sports and other activities.

items.sentence: ___there will be an ever-increasing need to provide for people at (9) play / recreation / rest.
items.explanation: 'At play' refers to people engaging in games or fun activities.

items.sentence: If the future is not one of increased leisure, then it won't be for (10) need / desire / want of demand.
items.explanation: 'For want of' is an idiom meaning 'due to a lack of'.

exercises.label: K
exercises.instruction: For each question, write one word which can be used in all three sentences.

items.sentence: To our ___, the boss said that we could have an extra day off work. / Martin always takes great ___ in talking to anyone about fishing. / When Erin finally started to move downhill on the skis for the first time, she laughed in ___.
items.explanation: The word 'delight' fits in 'to our delight', 'take delight in', and 'laugh in delight'.

items.sentence: It's important that people feel they can exercise ___ over their leisure activities. / Your fee is so late that I'm afraid we have no ___ but to cancel your membership. / The college offers a wide ___ of evening and weekend classes.
items.explanation: The word 'choice' fits in 'exercise choice', 'have no choice but', and 'wide choice'.

items.sentence: The local council should start a building programme to meet the ___ for leisure facilities. / The Mayor said he had no ___ of advice from people who didn't understand the situation. / This area is badly in ___ of financial investment.
items.explanation: The word 'need' fits in 'meet the need', 'have no need of', and 'in need of'.

items.sentence: I had dried frog when I was in Korea, and I would describe the ___ as unforgettable. / I thought that what you said to Erica was in very bad ___. / You might not like sushi the first time you try it - it's a bit of an acquired ___.
items.explanation: The word 'taste' fits 'the taste as unforgettable', 'in very bad taste', and 'acquired taste'.

items.sentence: We're quite ___ for our son to go to Cambridge University when he finishes school. / I enjoy team sports, like basketball and volleyball, and I'm especially ___ on football. / My parents have always taken a ___ interest in my hobbies.
items.explanation: The word 'keen' fits 'keen for someone to', 'keen on', and 'keen interest'.

items.sentence: I was a bit of a loner when I was younger and I spent a lot of time ___ by myself. / The government has been accused of ___ for time because it doesn't know what to do. / Liam never learnt to read music, but he's capable of ___ practically anything by ear.
items.explanation: The word 'playing' fits 'playing by myself', 'playing for time', and 'playing by ear'.

items.sentence: The headmaster was full of ___ for the school cricket team, who had won six matches in a row. / The police officers involved deserve ___ for their bravery in such a difficult situation. / If I read one more newspaper editorial in ___ of the Prime Minister I shall scream.
items.explanation: The word 'praise' fits 'full of praise', 'deserve praise', and 'in praise of'.

items.sentence: When we finally made it out of the forest, we decided to ___ before moving on. / Just ___ your bike against the gate and come inside. / You can ___ assured that I will not stop until I find the person who damaged the school gym.
items.explanation: The word 'rest' fits 'decided to rest', 'rest your bike against', and 'rest assured'.

exercises.label: L
exercises.instruction: Complete using the words in the box.

items.sentence: 'Oh! I'm exhausted! I've been doing housework all day.' 'Come and put your ___ up for five minutes and I'll make a cup of tea.'
items.explanation: The idiom 'put your feet up' means to relax.

items.sentence: 'Do you fancy going to that new jazz club in town?' 'Yes, let's go and let our ___ down a bit, shall we?'
items.explanation: The idiom 'let your hair down' means to relax and enjoy yourself.

items.sentence: 'Jamie is so much fun!' 'Yes, he really is the life and soul of the ___.'
items.explanation: The 'life and soul of the party' is someone very lively at social events.

items.sentence: 'You're not changing jobs again already, are you?' 'Well, they do say that variety is the ___ of life.'
items.explanation: The proverb 'variety is the spice of life' means different experiences make life interesting.

items.sentence: 'Roger wasn't pleased to get rejected by the team, was he?' 'No. He really had his ___ set on playing for them.'
items.explanation: The idiom 'set your heart on' means to want something very much.

items.sentence: 'You look a bit bored.' 'Well, I've been at a loose ___ since I finished the book I was reading.'
items.explanation: The idiom 'at a loose end' means having nothing particular to do.

items.sentence: 'You weren't planning to go to Singapore on holiday, were you?' 'No. I got to the travel agent's and just decided to go there on a ___.'
items.explanation: Doing something 'on a whim' means doing it because of a sudden feeling.

items.sentence: 'I can't believe you built the Eiffel Tower out of matchsticks!' 'I guess I must have had a lot of ___ on my hands.'
items.explanation: Having 'time on your hands' means having more time than you need.

items.sentence: 'Does Jenny ever move from in front of the TV?' 'I think she's in danger of becoming a bit of a couch ___.'
items.explanation: A 'couch potato' is someone who spends a lot of time sitting and watching TV.

items.sentence: 'You've got a great suntan!' 'I went away for a few days to recharge my ___.'
items.explanation: The idiom 'recharge your batteries' means to rest so you are ready to work again.

exercises.label: M
exercises.instruction: Complete the sentences by changing the form of the word in capitals.

items.sentence: The cricket ground and the theatre make this a very ___ (DESIRE) area to live in.
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'desire'.

items.sentence: My family have always gone in for traditional country ___ (PURSUE), such as hunting, shooting and fishing.
items.explanation: Plural noun form of 'pursue', meaning leisure activities.

items.sentence: What I like about Stravinsky's music is the ___ (RHYTHM) complexity, which has a powerful effect on the listener.
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'rhythm' (also 'rhythmical').

items.sentence: After ten matches, we had the ___ (ENVY) record of played ten, won none, lost ten.
items.explanation: The prefix 'un-' is needed because the record is bad, not to be envied.

items.sentence: I know you'd rather take your holiday in August, but when the boss is offering you extra days off, I don't think you can afford to be so ___ (CHOOSE).
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'choose', meaning difficult to please.

items.sentence: You don't need to be quite so ___ (ZEAL) in your work, and you might find you enjoy it more if you take a little time off.
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'zeal'.

items.sentence: You don't have time to do all the sports you'd like to, so you're going to have to be a bit ___ (SELECT).
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'select'.

items.sentence: After dinner, we went for a ___ (LEISURE) stroll along the seafront.
items.explanation: Adjective/adverb form of 'leisure', meaning relaxed and unhurried.

items.sentence: We're only two weeks into the school holidays and already my children are ___ (REST).
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'rest' meaning unable to stay still or quiet.

items.sentence: I don't mind Annabel spending time on her computer, but playing this latest game has become almost an ___ (OBSESS).
items.explanation: Noun form of 'obsess'.

exercises.label: N
exercises.instruction: Write a word formed from the words in the box in each gap.

items.sentence: It's impossible to (1) ___ (PLAY) the importance of getting active as a family.
items.explanation: In this context, it means one cannot exaggerate its importance.

items.sentence: We all have a (2) ___ (TEND) to start off with good intentions___
items.explanation: Noun form of 'tend'.

items.sentence: ___and then lose our (3) ___ (MOTIVE).
items.explanation: Noun form of 'motive'.

items.sentence: Playing and exercising together as a family can help you through those moments when you are (4) ___ (SATISFY) with your progress.
items.explanation: Negative adjective form of 'satisfy'.

items.sentence: Don't (5) ___ (DO) it. Start with gentle exercise and work up to something more strenuous.
items.explanation: Verb meaning to do something in excess.

items.sentence: Whatever your (6) ___ (PREFER) activity, make sure that you're all comfortable doing it at that level.
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'prefer'.

items.sentence: If necessary, switch to a more (7) ___ (RELAX) activity until you're all in better shape.
items.explanation: Adjective form of 'relax' describing an activity.

items.sentence: Children these days get a lot of (8) ___ (STIMULATE) from the fast-moving worlds of television, the Internet and computer games.
items.explanation: Noun form of 'stimulate'.

items.sentence: Contact Marlston Council to find out about the (9) ___ (AVAILABLE) of facilities where you live.
items.explanation: Noun form of 'available'.

items.sentence: Playing a sport together will teach your children about fair play, (10) ___ (SPORT) and working with others.
items.explanation: Noun referring to fair and generous behavior in sports.

exercises.label: O
exercises.instruction: Some verbs form nouns using -ence, as in preference. Some form nouns using -ance. Put nouns formed from the following words in the correct category.

items.sentence: accept
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: attend
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: differ
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: ignore
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: allow
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: avoid
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: disturb
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: inherit
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: annoy
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: clear
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: endure
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: insist
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: appear
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: correspond
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: exist
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: insure
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: assist
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: depend
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: guide
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: interfere
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: occur
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: perform
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: persist
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: reassure
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: refer
items.explanation: -ence

items.sentence: rely
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: resemble
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: resist
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: tolerate
items.explanation: -ance

items.sentence: utter
items.explanation: -ance

exercises.label: P
exercises.instruction: Complete the sentences using words formed in exercise O.

items.sentence: Do you think you could help me? I'm having real problems with this and I think I need some ___.
items.explanation: Noun from 'assist'.

items.sentence: There's such a close ___ between Mikey and his father, don't you think?
items.explanation: Noun from 'resemble'.

items.sentence: I use the Internet quite a bit now for looking up information and I don't really use the ___ books on my shelves.
items.explanation: Noun from 'refer', used as an adjective here.

items.sentence: The head teacher was walking past the classroom door when she heard a loud ___ coming from inside.
items.explanation: Noun from 'disturb'.

items.sentence: You need to be really fit to be able to complete an ___ race like the marathon.
items.explanation: Noun from 'endure', used as an adjective here.

items.sentence: You might feel like giving up, but keep practising and you'll be amazed at what a little ___ can achieve.
items.explanation: Noun from 'persist'.

items.sentence: This football team has a proud tradition and has been in ___ since 1890.
items.explanation: Noun from 'exist'.

items.sentence: There was a lot of ___ from local people to the government's plans to knock down the stadium.
items.explanation: Noun from 'resist'.
